speaker: interviewer
text: This letter that I have, that we have from other people-- as soon as you're-- don't wait for me, Crane. I want to get started. We're just yakking here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This letter that came to the Foundation from someone in the Stark who-- from Canton, who has family, who live on Superior. The legs were just amputated of an aunt because of diabetes. And they're related to Donald and Magda Neuberger.

speaker: interviewee
text: Donald and Magda were here last night.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to interview Magda in the summer. She's a survivor too.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she's not a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: She came here 1939. She escaped before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she's-- good. Oh, I would like to talk to her. See, we-- I interviewed a woman from Germany who escaped in '39.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But she was raised in Germany. And she gave-- she had amazing stories, because she was-- she was a-- and she saw Hitler three times.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Magda didn't have much of a--

speaker: interviewer
text: She didn't? No?

speaker: interviewee
text: She'll talk about her-- I figured-- I don't know why she's saying it. I too, was curious, but I didn't want to interrupt her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. I didn't want to say anything to her, because it's like, she knows what she's doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you just want to roll-- do you want to roll over what I just did?

words.text: Do you just want to roll-- do

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm doing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're rolling, tape speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. The survivor is Betty Gold. The language is English. The city is University Heights, the state Ohio, the country the United States of America. And today's date is Sunday, April 16th, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looks good, sounds good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Am I OK right here?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Meryl Goldberg. And I am an interviewer conducting an interview with Betty Gold. Today's date is Sunday, April 16th, 1995. And we are in University Heights, Ohio, in the United States of America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you please tell me your full-- your full name now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Betty Gold.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name you were born with? What was your full name, including your maiden name, that you were born with, in Polish or Yiddish, however?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basia Ruchel Potash.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Basia Ruchel Potash for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-A-S-I-A R-U-C-H-E-L Potash, P-O-T-A-S-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your complete birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: August 10, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city and country were you born? And if you could, could you spell the names for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zofjuwka, Poland. It was also called Trochenbrod. And Zofjuwka is spelled Z-O-F-J-U-W-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why it was called Trochenbrod?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the Yiddish name for the shtetl.

speaker: interviewer
text: One other thing. Did you have a nickname while you were growing up at all? Or did everyone call you Basia?

speaker: interviewee
text: They called me Basiula affectionately.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. So you were born in 1930.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I know you can't really remember that day. But if you could describe for me what Zofjuwka-- or do you want me to call it Trochenbrod? How would you-- what do you think would be best for us to refer to it as?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love it as Trochenbrod.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can you describe for me, from the time you remember, a little bit about Trochenbrod?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trochenbrod was a small shtetl, about 6,000 people. And all of them were Jewish except for the post lady, the gentleman who sold schnapps, and another gentleman who was busy putting out the fire on Shabbos and doing things for the Jewish people that they were not allowed to do on the Shabbos. And the rest of the population was Jewish. We were surrounded by Polish villages and Ukrainians. And it was a one-street shtetl. Everyone knew everyone and everyone was related to everyone. And we all lived very close to our aunts and uncles, just crossing the streets or walking next door. And it was-- they were mostly poor people, but there were some people that did very nicely, also, like my father did very well. And we have very nice house that he built. And I remember when they started to build in 1933. And it was furnished nicely. And he-- he provided a nice living. He was in the leather business. And he supplied the villagers with their shoes and boots and leather goods. And that's how he made a living. And of course, everyone's social life was in the shul or visiting each other. And it was a very close-knit, beloved shtetl. It was just great.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I interrupt for a minute?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. We'll just continue--

speaker: interviewer
text: We are rolling, yes. Unless you'd like--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. No. We'll keep going from where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You said there were 6,000 people. I'm now a little confused. You said to me there were 6,000 Jews. That seems like an awfully big shtetl to me. And you said it was a one-- a one-street-- so were there really 6,000 Jew-- were there 6,000 people and the majority of the people were Jewish? Just can you clarify that for me a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say-- I may be off a little bit. But the most would be 6,000. But they were all Jewish, all Jewish. It was a very, very long street. And it was called Trochenbrod. Then about two miles away, there was another little street and that was called [NON-ENGLISH]. But it was all Trochenbrod. So if you put all those three little towns together, it made up the entire shtetl.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you were looking-- if you were to look at a map of Poland--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where would you place Trochenbrod, if you looked-- you know, if you know where Warsaw is, in--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], Wolyn, that was the state.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it southeast, northwest--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's in-- OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you tell me a little bit about your own immediate family, your father's name, your mother's name, your grandparents, your brothers? Tell me about your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember my father's parents, my grandparents on my father's side and their children. And it was my father, and he had two brothers and two sisters. And my mother had-- I don't remember my mother's father, because he died when-- when my mother was three years old. But I remember my grandmother, her mother. And she lived alone across the street. And we used to love to go there and sleep there and live with her and so forth. And they had seven children. There were one, two, three, four sisters and three brothers. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] then I had two brothers. My older brother was born in 1923. He name was Shimon. And my younger brother-- he's older than I am. He was older than I am, but younger than my older brother. He was born in 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And his name is-- was Bernard, Boruch. And I was the baby. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] my older brother and I were seven years apart. And he was a very tall young man. And he's the only one that we lost during the Holocaust. Just almost towards the end of the war, unfortunately. And then my younger brother and I and my parents survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your mother. What was-- did she work? What did she-- was she a housewife? Did she work with your father? What-- what kind of house did she keep?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-- my mother had a very nice life. She was-- they considered her the society lady in the shtetl. She was involved in [NON-ENGLISH] and she was involved in Jewish organizationals. And she would write someplace. And she always was very, very well groomed. And she was really very, very beautiful. And she took a great deal of pride in how she presented herself to-- to the people in the shtetl. And she never worked because my father really provided well for her. And it was-- she didn't have to. And it wasn't just fashionable, we don't-- I don't remember women working when I was a little girl at all. I don't remember one person in the shtetl working, as far as women are concerned, unless their husbands had a business and they helped him out. Otherwise, I don't know of any women that worked, that I remember. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she kept a very, very busy life. And it was very nice. It was very nice. My father worked in the business. And I loved my dad. And I used to go on the way to school we'd go together. And he'd go to the shop and I'd continue to school and stop at the bakery on the way and buy a [NON-ENGLISH], charge it to my father. [LAUGHS] And it was wonderful. It was really great. They had a-- they had a pretty decent life until the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: But during while this nice time, while this good time, tell me about Jewish life. Did you come from a religious family, an assimilated family? Tell me about Jewish life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came from an Orthodox family, Modern Orthodox. Most of the people in our shtetl were Orthodox. We didn't have any Reformed or Conservative. You were either an Orthodox or you were a goy, they called you. So-- and all the-- we had two temples and they were both Orthodox. And we observed all the rules and regulations as far as [NON-ENGLISH] was concerned. And I went to [NON-ENGLISH] besides going to regular school. And my brothers went to-- also to [NON-ENGLISH] and plus school. We had a private tutor come in the house, teach us Hebrew. We spoke fluently Hebrew. We started lessons in Hebrew when we were like four years old, all of us, all three kids. They gave us a pretty good education for youngsters as long as possible until the war started. The best part, of course, that I loved, were-- were when all the families, you know, would be together and visit and go to the park on Shabbos afternoon and singing the Shabbos songs. And I don't know, there was some kind of like-- the house used to light up when it came like Friday night before Shabbos. Everybody would be bathed and dressed, and lights were on, and the candles were shining, and-- I don't know. It was such an enjoyable time. And I wish I could enjoy it now as much, but I can't. It just can't be the same. I don't know why. I miss it terribly. And especially the family, the visitation, and-- and being together. It's just such a unity. It's very difficult to explain. And some people really struggled and they were poorer. But there's tons and tons of love and caring. And the children were so loved, and the mothers. And there was much respect and [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a lot of strictness, also. Like my father would always say, you know-- if I would say, Dad, what would you do if I'd go out with a Gentile boy? He says, in my house, we don't ask questions like these. I mean, you know, that was it. No explanation. And they really didn't have the know-how as far as psychology was concerned and all that. They just spoke from their heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were good parents. And-- and, oh, I'm sure we had our difficulties as children, as far as behavior is concerned, or whatever. But we really enjoyed it, very close-knit family. It was lovely. I remember the weddings. I used to sing at the weddings when I was a little girl. They would be outside. And everybody attended. I mean, the entire shtetl attended. And I always-- I don't know why they picked me to say the [NON-ENGLISH] or the song or the poem. I would read for the-- just like now when you walk down the aisle, there's music, somebody sang or read a poem or something. And I always enjoyed doing that outside and I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of those?

speaker: interviewee
text: I probably remember a couple. But I don't-- I wouldn't take a chance--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you recite one?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, it would be so nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had it written down, but I can't find it. So I remember those wonderful wonderful weddings. I enjoyed them. And I also participated with my brother in a lot of children's plays in school as well as in the organization, the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH] and organization. And I enjoyed that very much. And we had lots of cousins. And a cousin wasn't a cousin in Europe. A cousin was a sister or a brother. There was no difference. We'd live together. We slept so often together and played together, went to school together. We didn't know the difference if they were sisters, brothers, or cousins. It-- it was very, very nice. Of course, there was a lot of poverty there, too. But it-- it's interesting that those who had helped the others. I remember that almost-- at least twice a month, every Friday, I would go with a girlfriend of mine and we'd both carry a basket. And we'd go from house to house and collect baked goods and-- and pastries and candy or whatever one could give. And we-- then we'd take it to the poor people. And I started doing that when I was a very little girl. And a lot of children did it in Europe, in our shtetl, anyway. And that was-- I looked forward to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, of course, I would always steal much more from our house. And when I'd start off, I'd have a big beginning already. And my mother would say, what happened to the [NON-ENGLISH]? What happened to that or that? I really enjoyed doing that

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I liked to go with my father to the big city when he went to buy leather and stuff. He would take me along and I enjoyed that very much. And I always dreamt of being in the big city. I looked at the buildings and the people and some cars, and it was so exciting. And I dreamt that, well, when I grow up, that's what I'm going to do. I'm going to live in a big city. And I'm going to go to the gymnasium in the big city, and so on and on and on. And so I did have a-- I really had a nice childhood. I enjoyed it very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the big city you went to with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lutsk. It wasn't that big. But to me, it was big in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your shtetl, in-- at the time you were born and those first few years, do you remember were there cars? Were there-- was it horse and buggy? What-- can you describe the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no cars. There were-- there were a lot of merchants who had horses. and they would go to market. And they would sell either cattle or buy cattle or sell fruits and vegetables or buy fruits and vegetables, trade. They were traders or barters. I don't know what you call them in those days. But they-- they did a lot of that. Going to market was the big, big thing for, I would say, 50% of the people in town went to market. I don't know what they did so much at market. I think they must have exported more than imported, because they worked the land. Everyone had land. And they--they had their own fruits and vegetables and-- and big gardens, acres, not just a little bit. And besides providing food for their families, they had a lot to sell, so they used to take the wares to the market and make money this way. And also, they-- a lot of people had cows. And they would take the milk. It would be picked up to make butter and cheese and so forth, so they made a living of that. And we had a goat. And I had chickens. And I even had a horse at one time that I loved to ride, no saddle, of course. And I remember in 1933, an aunt and uncle of mine came to visit us in the shtetl. And of course, we had a big open house and whatnot. And we took a picture of everyone.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what do you think happens in the middle of taking a picture? I mean, the cow would come and leave some droppings. And I remember that. I don't know why. [LAUGHS] It really funny, in our backyard. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know what else to tell you. There was so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were bad times too, because there was no immediate doctor to go to. You had to go really very far. And the pharmacist, it was an apteka called. Pharmacy was an apteka. And he wasn't even a pharmacist. I don't know what you would call him. And he would provide medicine and treatment. A felcher, that's what they called him, a felcher for minor illnesses. But when someone was really ill, it was dangerous, because it took a long time to get them on a horse and a wagon to the city. We couldn't call a cab. We couldn't call car service. And I remember when I was little, there was no electricity in our-- in our shtetl, and there were no paved roads. And then we had-- years later, I remember, maybe five years, maybe I was five, six years old-- I remember they were building-- the roads were paved. And we had electricity. And they begin to develop more and more and more, you know what I mean. So-- but for a long time, it was very, very hard. They had some cars would start coming into the city, and life, communication began to happen. My father bought a radio. And that was so exciting. The whole town came to see how it's being installed and the wires going into the ground or whatever. I remember them working on it. And we had a phonograph and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember first hearing on the radio? Do you at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember-- yes, I do. I remember Polish music. And then I remember-- I think it was Jozef Pilsudski, who was the President, and he was making a speech of some sort. I don't remember what he said, of course. But I remember the name, oh, Jozef Pilsudski is going to be-- Jozef Pilsudski is going to speak. That's when I heard-- first time I heard about Jozef Pilsudski. But the first thing I heard was some Polish music. It was very, very exciting. And I remember like my eighth birthday party. I think it was my only birthday party. I remember my little friends came.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one brought me, for a present, a bobby pin. I mean, can you imagine, a bobby pin? It probably cost two cents. One would bring me a ribbon. And one brought me some very pretty, colorful rubber bands or-- I mean, it's all relative. Can you imagine today compared to--

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was so exciting. I think I-- I was more fond of that than if I would get today a $1,000 gift. It was so nice and, I don't know, such good feelings. I remember being so happy. I loved it. And-- but I think that was about the only birthday party I ever had. I also remember my brothers' bar mitzvah. It was so unlike now. Went to shul and-- the women didn't go to shul that much on Shabbos just the men and the sons and-- And after shul, they came to the house, and we had [NON-ENGLISH], sponge cake and whiskey and wine and fruit and so forth. It was, you know, nothing like this. I also remember-- very important then that stands out in my mind is my father wrote-- he-- he brought a-- a rabbi. I don't remember from what city he was. But he was a very famous rabbi. And we housed him in our house like for three weeks. And my father dedicated the Sefer Torah to the shul. And the scribe was there writing it. And people would come and donate whatever they could. And the scribe would write a paragraph, or a sentence, or a word-- I don't know-- and their name. It meant a great deal to everyone. And my father put me on top of the closet. We had like a closet, armoire, they call them now. And I was sitting on top of the closet, and I was watching the people come in and make their donations, their dollar or $0.50 or $0.10, Russian, you know, Polish money. And I would sit on top of the closet with my brother, or sometimes alone, and watch the people come in and go out and go in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was-- I couldn't figure out what it was. And then-- then surprisingly, the rabbi-- and I remember him with a beard and big, fur shtreimel and whatnot. He explained it to me, how it works. And I was so fascinated, you know? And so that was a very, very memorable time in my life. I remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I also remember like my aunt and uncle came from-- no, I think it was just my aunt without her uncle-- without her husband came to visit in 1933. And we had the biggest, biggest party in town in her honor. I mean-- and this is an aunt that left when she was 17 or 16 years old for the United States. And she came to visit just before the-- you know, 1933. And that was the biggest party in town to honor my aunt from America. I mean, everybody came out of the closet. [LAUGHS] She was like, today, a big celebrity, maybe like O.J. Simpson. And everybody gathered and it was so much fun. It was just delightful. It was wonderful. And I was sitting there listening and asking her stories about America. I was a little girl, you know, 4 years old, or whatever. And I was so curious.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember her telling me that there-- there are black people in America. And I didn't know what she was talking about. And I kept bothering her, bothering her until she left. What's a black person? What's a black-- and she didn't have a picture to show me. I was so curious.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just had to find out what she was talking about. And of course, it wasn't 'til-- 'til after the war that I met one. But those are memories. Those are memories and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about the synagogues and how big they were and if there were some synagogues in houses as well. You know, like the real-- like the real, real Hasidim. I'm sure you had Hasidim in your town as well as the Orthodox.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had Hasidim. And we had Yeshiva Bachurim. They used to come and people would house them in their homes. We had two synagogues. One was ultra-Orthodox and was like four houses away from us. And the rabbi and his wife and children lived there. And the synagogue was in the same house. It was a big, big house. And they lived in the back, and the synagogue was in the front. My father belonged to both of them. The other synagogue was uptown, in a better neighborhood maybe half a mile away. [LAUGHS] And it was a little more conservative. Not conservative at all, by all means, but they were not as strict as this ultra synagogue.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the women would sit upstairs. And I would always come during services to visit my mother upstairs and-- mostly during the holidays. On Shabbos the women didn't go much to synagogue at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we would-- my mother and I would wait at home and prepare the Shabbos meal in the afternoon, after they would come back shul. And then they take a nap, my parents. And after the nap, we would take a walk to the park. And Jewish people would meet there and sit, and gossip and whatnot, talk politics, and small talk. And all the children would play together. But in the synagogue it was-- it was very chauvinistic. I mean, it was just men. Women really didn't have any say. It was-- they were not important at all. They were just-- they were the enablers, the supporters. And they were very Orthodox, like I say. And I was a very, very Orthodox child. I was the most Orthodox person in my family. My mother would comb her hair on Shabbos, I would squeal on her to my dad. I would say, Mom combed her-- I was so upset. It would upset me to no end. I thought, you know, she would drop dead for doing it. And if my brother would do a little homework or break a stick or something, I-- I would be squealing. I would hit him. I would be so upset. I'd cry. I was worried about him. But my mother wasn't-- I was a fanatic, because-- I don't know, I was taught that way. And I figured, what is it-- that's what you're supposed to do. They broke the law as far as I was concerned. So-- but most of the shtetl, 95% were like that. And the other synagogue was a little more elite. It was a brick-- a brick building. The only brick building that I remember in the shtetl was that other synagogue. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had a chevra kadisha. They-- they really did a lot for small town, in a poor little shtetl like that. They accomplished a lot. They would bring poor people in for Shabbos. There were always guests for Shabbos. They were travelers, merchants on the road passing by and they couldn't make it home in time. Everybody had to bring somebody home from shul to the table. So we always met a lot of different-- mostly men, of course, from different towns and villages and cities. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were always-- thought about tzedekah and charity and it was very important in our lives. We always had the [NON-ENGLISH] and otherwise. And my parents-- even though I had a lot of family who immigrated to the States, my-- like my mother's sisters and brother and so forth, they used to send us some packages once in a while, five zlotys or-- we really didn't need it that bad. But we accepted it and we gave it to our poorer relatives when it came. So we didn't discourage them from sending those things. But we would give it-- my father would always give it to our poorer families, and-- and my, you know, his sisters or brother or nephews and nieces who needed it more than we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: You indicated that-- that people came through and stayed in your home. Do you remember-- and did this even happen in Trochenbrod? Once Hitler came to power in '33-- I'm going to ask you a question now, and then you're going to respond at the beginning of the next tape. Do you remember any Volksdeutsche? Do you remember people coming through from Germany? OK. That's what we're going to talk about. We're going to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Then we'll talk-- when we--
